entry.title: Agentic Memory Unpacked: Families, Types, Architectures, and Lifecycle
entry.content:
In my previous article, I gave an overview of agentic memory — what it is, when to use it, and how it relates to learning. This article delves deeper into memory families, content types, organizational architectures, and the memory lifecycle. After reading it, you will be equipped with all the knowledge and vocabulary necessary to start your own memory experiments and step up as a competent discussion partner on the matter.
 
This article draws heavily on Memory in the Age of AI Agents: A survey, augmented by my own thoughts and examples. Memory in the Age of AI Agents is a great (and very long) read that paints a comprehensive picture of agentic memory research. This post can't substitute the full paper, but I hope it provides a practical starting point — and I invite you to adopt its taxonomies and vocabulary.
 

Memory Families: Where and how do agents store memories?

 

Vanilla LLMs are stateless across separate interactions. This observation serves as the basis for all discussions of memory. However, it is important to note that this does not mean that LLM cannot store knowledge. In fact, everything an LLM "knows" is encoded in its model weights. It is thus logical to consider continuous adaptation of model weights during runtime to store memories and insights on user and environment interactions. Because this strategy encodes memory information in model parameters, it is also known as parametric memory.
 
Another form of memory that lives close to the core of the LLM is latent memory. Latent memory is formed not in the model weights, but in its hidden states and caches that can be snapshotted, persisted, and loaded back into the next session. Both parametric and latent memory are advanced concepts that require deeper model understanding; future posts will cover them.

Finally, token-level memory refers to memory created and persisted in various forms outside the LLM, which is then injected into the context window of a new session as tokens.
 

 Token-level, Parametric, and Latent Memory:  Description and Analogy
Memory FamilyDescriptionHuman Analogy
Parametric memoryKnowledge encoded directly in the model's weights through training or fine-tuning. For example, a model fine-tuned on company-specific terminology and workflowsLearned and deeply internalized skills like riding a bike — you don't consult a manual, you know
Latent memoryInternal representations, such as hidden states or caches that can be persisted and restored. For example, a snapshotted KV cache from a prior session or compressed hidden state checkpoints.The "mental context" you hold while working on a problem — lose focus, and it's gone
Token-level memory
Explicit, discrete memory stored externally and injected as tokens into the LLM context window. Examples include conversation logs, user preference files, shared documents, and knowledge snippets retrieved from dedicated storage structures.
A notebook you carry to note down and develop thoughts and observations



Due to the nature of these memory families, they come with some inherent pros and cons:


Token-level, Parametric, and Latent Memory:  Comparison 
PropertyToken-level MemoryParametric & Latent Memory
AuditabilityAuditable, potentially human-readable, symbolicNon-auditable, non-human-readable
ExplainabilityHigh — memories can be inspected and understood directlyLow — individual updates are only meaningful within the context of all other weights
EfficiencyRetrieval overhead and increased latency during inferencePrincipally higher efficiency; no retrieval step needed
ReliabilityAdditional RAG link in the chain makes end-to-end pipeline potentially less reliableKnowledge is embedded in the model itself; similar reliability to world facts in LLM
Measurability
Full scan and inspection of stored knowledge is possibleCan only be measured by probing; no meaningful full scan
Natural fit
Tasks that need auditing, high-level strategies (type 2 thinking - deliberate, analytical)Fuzzy tasks like image recognition and control; tasks that escape explicit rule sets (type 1 thinking — fast, intuitive)

 

The remainder of this article focuses exclusively on the most intuitive and actively discussed memory family: token-level memory.
 

Memory Contents: What do we store?

 

My previous article discussed basic considerations regarding agentic memory use cases, but knowing when to use agentic memory is one thing; knowing what to store is another.

To delineate which types of content can be stored in agentic memory, I find it helpful to look at memory models. Drawing on a background of psychology and neuroscience, Endel Tulving proposed a memory model originally intended to describe human memory. This model is now often referenced in AI. Tulving's model distinguishes "semantic", "procedural", and "episodic memory". A slightly different but overlapping model is put forward in Memory in the Age of AI Agents. In it, "factual memory", "experiential memory", and "working memory" are distinguished.

 
Memory Models: Types, Subtypes, Description, and Examples
Lifespan & Memory TypeTulving EquivalentSubtypeDescriptionExample
Long-term:
Factual		SemanticUser factualFacts and preferences about the user."The user prefers concise answers."
  Environment factualFacts about the environment, domain, or world the agent operates in."The production database uses PostgreSQL 15."
Long-term:
Experiential		Procedural + EpisodicCase-basedMinimally processed records of historical episodes. Retain specific context and outcomes.A full interaction log of a debugging session that resolved a timeout error.
  Strategy-basedDistilled, transferable reasoning patterns, workflows, and high-level insights from past trajectories. Decouple decision-making from specific contexts."When facing erratic tests, first check for shared state between test cases before investigating timing issues."
  Skills-basedExecutable procedural capacities like atomic code snippets or other artifacts that operationalize abstract strategies.A reusable shell script for rotating log files, derived from repeated manual operations.
Short-term: Working——Capacity-limited, dynamically controlled collection of information for active context management during a single task or session. Erased after the session ends. The current conversation history, to-do lists, or scratchpad notes.
 

Notice that no strict boundaries exist between these types: memory architectures may or may not physically separate them, and one type can eventually be derived from the other. For example, information stored in working and episodic memory may be abstracted and condensed to form long-term semantic or procedural memories.
 

Information Architectures: How are memories organized?

 

Independent of the type of individual memories, they may be related; like a cluster of user preferences for text formatting (semantic), a set of events with a temporal history (episodic), or a bunch of code snippets for interacting with an external data source (procedural). The recent popularity of knowledge graphs is a testament to the importance of such relations in many agentic applications, prompting us to consider how memories may be connected.

The following are prototypic forms of organizations that an agentic memory corpus may have. In my opinion, it is helpful to distinguish and name them, because they are the perfect bridge between what we expect the memory to do and how we implement it structurally:
  • Flat Memory (1D): No explicit inter-unit topology. Memories are accumulated as sequences or bags of units (e.g., text snippets, trajectories, chunks). They may reference one another implicitly, e.g., "as known from a recent conversation".
  • Planar Memory (2D): A structured but single-layer organization within one plane: units are related by a graph, tree, table, and so on, with no cross-layer relations. The structure is explicit, but not layered (e.g., a single knowledge graph).
  • Hierarchical Memory (3D): Structured across multiple layers with inter-layer links, forming a volumetric or stratified memory. (e.g., multiple knowledge graphs at different levels of abstraction for layered retrieval.)

Just a few days ago, Andrej Karpathy shared his view on the importance of relations among agentic memories under the concept of an "LLM Wiki". Irrespective of whether we call this dynamic knowledge base a wiki or a memory corpus, the importance of cross-referencing in many practical applications is clear, as is the goal of creating and maintaining "a persistent, compounding artifact" of connected information.
 

Memory Lifecycle: How does it all come together? - A cradle-to-grave memory perspective

 

Bearing potential content and organizational structures in mind greatly helps articulate the purpose of an agentic memory system and begin drafting an implementation architecture. There is, however, another perspective that influences design: the memory lifecycle — from creation through alteration to eventual deletion.

The following are the three main lifecycle phases of agentic memory:
  • Formation – Capturing novel insights from interactions and experiences.
  • Retrieval – Intelligently surfacing relevant information to enhance the agent's context and decision-making for future tasks.
  • Evolution – Refining, synthesizing, and connecting stored knowledge through reflection.


Memory Formation "How to extract the memory (from traces)?"

Agentic memory forms from observable artifacts — agent logs and traces — but memories are typically more refined than raw logs. Forming them involves processing steps like the following:
  • Semantic Summarization is a lossy compression mechanism, distilling the gist of lengthy interaction logs. It does not interpret or refine the contained information other than by reducing noise and compressing it as is. Semantic summarization reduces context length, allowing more core insights to be loaded into a single LLM context window upon retrieval. Also, this form of summarization may help LLM focus on important parts of a log.
  • Knowledge Distillation aims to extract higher-order reusable knowledge from interaction trajectories and artifacts. This may involve relating a current episode to prior learning signals and records to spot a pattern carrying reusable knowledge, e.g., "this is the 10th time I am being asked to refactor a result to include docstrings, I will assume that the user always wants docstrings until further notice."
  • Structured Construction builds on top of memory units produced by semantic summarization and knowledge distillation. Structured construction is used to relate pieces of information to one another, i.e., to build a higher-order organization from flat memory units.

These processes often co-occur: processing a single interaction log might involve summarizing it, distilling a skill from recurring patterns, and linking that skill to related context descriptions for future retrieval.
 

Memory Retrieval "How to utilize memory?"

Agentic memory ultimately has to serve its purpose during runtime. At some point, an agent will be asked to provide help with a task that benefits from information contained in the memory corpus. Memory retrieval is fundamentally a RAG problem — the same metrics (accuracy, speed, cost) and techniques apply, just drawing from a memory-specific corpus. It is thus not a focus of this article. However, retrieval signals (e.g., frequency) can feed back into the evolution of memory, as you will see below.
 

Memory Evolution "How to refine the memory?"

Forming memories from agent traces provides the memory base with new insights. However, these insights may contradict, add to, or relate to prior memories. This is why long-running agentic memory solutions will inevitably need to offer ways to evolve memories over time, possibly even outside of active user sessions:
 

Memory Evolution Mechanisms: Purpose and Key Challenges
MechanismPurposeKey Challenge
ConsolidationMerge new and existing memories into generalized insights, ensuring cumulative learningDetermining the right granularity for matching and merging
UpdatingResolve conflicts between new and existing memories to maintain accuracy and relevanceStability-plasticity dilemma: when to overwrite vs. treat as noise
ForgettingRemove outdated or redundant information to maintain focus and efficiencyOverly aggressive pruning risks erasing rare but essential knowledge
 
 
In principle, humans can supply feedback or support throughout any of these lifecycle phases. E.g., humans can inspect the memory database and mark memories as outdated, update memories, or merge memories. This behavior is already implemented in solutions like ChatGPT's memory. In addition to manual and LLM work, deterministic rules can be used to evolve the memory base. E.g., memory forgetting can be modeled using deterministic measures such as time since creation or retrieval frequency.


What's Next?

 

This article, alongside its predecessor, serves as an introduction to agentic memory. Upcoming articles on the matter will focus on deep dives into topics like latent memory. With them, we are moving closer to the cutting edge of research and industry.
 
 
# CTO Research and Innovation Munich
@ladenaw

entry.title: SPROXY shows “Service outdated” only for newly created Integration Suite Service Interfaces
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

we are currently testing ABAP proxy generation with SAP Integration Suite.

We observed the following behavior in transaction SPROXY:

  • Migrated PI/PO service interfaces can be activated and do not show this issue
  • New service interfaces created directly in SAP Integration Suite can also be used to generate ABAP proxies
  • Runtime communication works: the ABAP proxy can receive messages from Integration Suite
  • However, in SPROXY these newly created interfaces remain in status “Service outdated”
  • Regeneration and activation of the proxy do not remove the status

 

So the issue seems to occur only for service interfaces modeled natively in Integration Suite, not for interfaces migrated from PI/PO.

Questions:

  1. Is this a known limitation or bug of ABAP proxy generation with newly created Integration Suite service interfaces?
  2. Can the “Service outdated” status be ignored if runtime communication is already working?
  3. Has anyone managed to get rid of this status for newly created interfaces?

Any experience would be greatly appreciated.

KR

Stefan

entry.title: Reuse a DTV (Data Transition Validation) Project in a New BTC Cycle
entry.content:
Overview

When you create a new cycle in the Manage Cycles app, the system automatically creates a new DTV project. In this situation, you may want to reuse the settings from an existing project.

Prerequisites

You created a new cycle in the Manage Cycles app and set the Run Data Transition Validation (DTV) Tool option to Yes.

Procedure

  1. In transaction DTV, export the project that has the settings you want to reuse.

  2. The system automatically creates a new DTV project when you create the cycle. In transaction DTV, locate this project and copy the project ID. This ID will be required in step 4.

    Note: The project ID is the same as the cycle ID that you can view in the Manage Cycles app.

  3. Change the project ID of the newly created project to a temporary name.

  4. Restart transaction DTV and import the project you exported in step 1, but specify the project ID you copied in step 2. This creates a new project with the project ID of your new cycle and that contains the configuration settings you want to reuse.

  5. (Optional) Delete the project you renamed in step 3.

entry.title: Re: Table names and string translation
entry.content:
Do You mean the second option with the stored procedure "TmSp_GetDefaultElementsforCR"?

No, you don't need any subreports. You only need to add the stored procedure "TmSp_GetDefaultElementsforCR" to the layout without linking it.

This procedure has two parameters: DocKey@ and ObjectId@. Therefore, the result of this procedure will be a single row with many columns (one column per text entry). The row contains the translations in the language of the document.

Here is the corresponding SAP note, where all of this is described:

https://me.sap.com/notes/2785541

This option (with the stored procedure "TmSp_GetDefaultElementsforCR") should be selected if the client requires translations in multiple languages. However, if only German and English are sufficient, I would recommend choosing the first option with variables for each text entry for performance reasons.

entry.title: 001 The "IDEA" in the Room
entry.content:
Two-thirds of business leaders say they don't fully trust their data. For organizations trying to deploy AI into critical workflows, that's not a statistic — it's a structural problem. The people closest to it — the data leaders, the architects, the CDOs navigating it daily — knew this long before the analysts did. And yet, until recently, nobody had built an institution to solve it.

This is the story of how one got started. And how you can get involved.

---

With an operational mind and background in accounting, I'm wired to see problems in terms of processes — where the money flows, where the logic breaks, where the gap between the model and the reality quietly widens until someone has to explain it to a board.

I grew up in Vancouver, built a career at SAP, and oddly I've spent more than a healthy amount of time thinking about institutions. The ones that exist because a problem was real enough, and persistent enough, that the people living inside it eventually decided to stop waiting for someone else to fix it.

That instinct had been with me for a long time. It took a dinner on a rainy Tuesday night in Vancouver to finally do something about it.

---

The first iteration surfaced around 2017. I was working inside SAP's New Ventures and Technology group — a genuinely progressive initiative that gave people like me the room to think commercially, take real risks, and build something with teeth. Along with David Brockington, I co-founded ADAZA by SAP there. Innovative work, interesting problems, and a front-row seat to the Canadian startup ecosystem as I began to build relationships with local accelerators.

What I saw was a talent community that was genuinely impressive in capability but often narrowly focused in ambition. A lot of energy going toward consumer apps and niche digital tools, while industries with massive, expensive, structurally complex problems sat a few hundred kilometers away. Forestry. Mining. Energy. Canada's economic backbone. The kind of industries where a well-aimed technology intervention doesn't just improve a conversion rate, it changes the economics of an entire operation.

The concept I kept returning to was a bridge. A proper institute — independent, practitioner-led — connecting the people who knew how to build with the industries that had something worth building for. Not a conference. Not a vendor program. A place where ideas met problems, and both sides left smarter.

It didn't happen. The timing wasn't right, the path wasn't clear, and I wasn't ready. But the shape of the idea stayed with me.

---

Fast forward to 2024. I'd spent the better part of six years leading operations for SAP's Business Technology Platform (BTP) marketing group, before becoming Chief of Staff to our CMO. Consequential work, but I'd been orchestrating someone else's programs for long enough. I wanted to own something again. Build something. Create direct impact, with a clear line from effort to outcome.

The vision of a think tank returned. This time, it had found the right problem.

I'd been watching the same conversation play out, over and over, across the data and AI landscape. Intelligent, experienced leaders — the people responsible for some of the most complex data environments on the planet — all carrying the same quiet frustration. Years of investment. Sophisticated platforms. Governance committees. And still, when it came time to actually trust the data underneath a consequential decision, there was a pause. A hesitation. A quiet but persistent gap between what the architecture was supposed to deliver and what the business actually experienced.

The numbers make the gap concrete. Nearly half of enterprises report active struggles with data fragmentation. Poor data quality costs the average organization $12.9 million per year — and that's the average. In a large, complex enterprise — a global bank, say, with decades of system accumulation, regulatory overlays, and acquired platforms that were never properly integrated — the figure is almost certainly higher, and largely untracked.

Meanwhile, AI had arrived at the party and was asking uncomfortable questions. Not about the technology — the models were impressive, the use cases were compelling. The problem was what sat underneath them. As one executive put it at a roundtable we later ran in Sydney: "98 percent of the world's data is not inside the large foundation models. It's locked in enterprises — in the systems." That's not a technology gap. That's a foundation problem.

Everyone was experimenting with AI. Copilots, generative tools, workflow automation — pilots were running in almost every organization we spoke with. But converting those experiments into scaled enterprise capability was proving elusive, particularly in regulated sectors where core business workflows depend on sensitive proprietary data, compliance requirements, and high-stakes decisions. Deploying AI into those environments requires something most organizations hadn't finished building: a data foundation they could actually trust.

The barrier wasn't the model. It was the meeting that had to happen before the model could be used. As another data leader at the same roundtable in Sydney put it rather directly: "The painful part for me is the governance around that… most of my time is going through all of the committees." Architecture review. Third-party risk. Responsible data governance bodies. Necessary, all of it — and collectively consuming the bandwidth of some of the sharpest people in the organization.

Scaling AI is less a technical challenge than a governance one. That line became a thesis.

---

What I couldn't find, in 2024, was a credible and independent space where senior leaders could sit down, be genuinely honest about this problem, and work through it together — across industries, across geographies, without a vendor agenda in the room. Analyst firms produce reports. Conferences produce panels. Vendors produce whitepapers. Nobody had built the institution.

Why does nobody own this?

That question had been sitting in the back of my mind for months when Dan Yu, our CMO for Data & Analytics, came to town.

Dan was in Vancouver for team meetings during what was, diplomatically, a period of organizational change. We carved out a Tuesday evening for dinner at Vij's, a local favourite just down the street. The kind of place where the food is good enough that you stop performing and just talk.

I didn't walk in with a deck. I walked in with a direction. A non-profit research organization dedicated to the advancement of data architecture and enterprise AI. Independent. Practitioner-led. Built on the insight that the problem wasn't technical — it was organizational, cultural, and structural. And that the only way to move the needle was to get the right people in a room, outside the commercial context, with enough trust to be honest about where things actually stood.

Dan had been part of establishing the TBM Council in a previous chapter of his career — a serious, respected organization that had done exactly this kind of work for technology business management. He understood the model. He understood what it took to build something that lasted. And he could see, as clearly as I could, that nothing like it existed for the data architecture conversation.

He didn't need much convincing.

We finished dinner. I walked home. And within a couple of weeks, I was writing up the plans.

---

There's a line from IDEA's first research brief that I keep coming back to: "The quality of your decisions will never exceed the quality of your data." Simple. Almost obvious. And yet most organizations are operating with a trust deficit they haven't fully priced. Companies that treat data as a strategic asset — not an IT problem — are 2.3 times more likely to grow revenue using AI. They make decisions 33% faster. They're not smarter. They've just fixed the foundation.

That was the gap IDEA was built to address. Not another vendor platform. Not another industry conference. An independent forum where the people responsible for enterprise data could work through the real problems together — and produce something the field could actually use.

What followed that dinner was the beginning of an experiment: could we build a genuinely independent space for the people responsible for the world's most complex data environments to solve this problem together? The roundtables, the research, the partnerships, the arguments about what the institution should and shouldn't be — that's what this is about.

---

*Next: Getting SAP to say yes — which, it turned out, was the easy part.*

---

**About IDEA**

IDEA — the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI — is a programme of research, community building, and peer learning, delivered in partnership with the Open Data Institute (ODI). The ODI brings 14 years of experience stewarding independent, multi-stakeholder research across public and private sectors. Together, we're building a space where enterprise data leaders can cut through the noise, share what's actually working, and help shape a vendor-agnostic blueprint for AI-ready data infrastructure.

The programme is actively seeking involvement from data leaders, industry partners, and academic experts. If you work at the intersection of data, governance, and enterprise AI — and you have something real to contribute — we'd like to hear from you.

--> Find out more and get involved

entry.title: Re: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent (Part 1 of 6)
entry.content:
@chetan_099 Thank you for your questions.

On the SDK package name — you are correct. generative-ai-hub-sdk is now deprecated and the replacement is pip install sap-ai-sdk-gen (SAP Cloud SDK for AI - Python). The good news is that class names are unchanged so all code in the series continues to work — only the installation command needs updating. I will revise the articles accordingly. Good catch.

On where to run the code and the LeanIX SAP AI agent hub question:

For the development environment, SAP BAS is the recommended option for interactive development and notebook-based exploration as covered in Part 2 of the Generative AI Hub series. For production, the agents in this series are designed to deploy on BTP Cloud Foundry as Flask or FastAPI applications.

On LeanIX — the SAP AI agent hub there is a governance and discovery layer, not an orchestration runtime. It helps organizations inventory and govern their AI agents across the enterprise. It doesn't replace LangChain or LangGraph — those still handle the reasoning, tool calling, and execution logic at runtime. Think of LeanIX as the control plane for agent governance and BTP as the runtime where agents actually execute. The two are complementary rather than competing, and your LangChain/LangGraph orchestration code is unaffected by it.
